---
title: "Dashboard Setup & Maintenance Guide"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "On this page"
    page-layout: article
    css: styles/dashboard.css
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
        <link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Fraunces:ital,opsz,wght@0,9..144,300;0,9..144,600&family=DM+Mono:wght@400&family=DM+Sans:wght@300;400;500&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
execute:
  echo: false
---

# Examples Dashboard — Complete Guide

This document covers everything you need to set up, populate, maintain, and extend the Examples Dashboard as a template repository owner or contributor.

---

## Repository Structure

```
your-repo/
│
├── dashboard.qmd                    ← Main dashboard page
├── instructions.qmd                 ← This guide
│
├── styles/
│   └── dashboard.css                ← All dashboard styling
│
├── content/
│   ├── examples.yaml                ← Single source of truth for all examples
│   └── card-images/                 ← Thumbnail/screenshot images
│       ├── placeholder.svg          ← Fallback shown when no image is set
│       └── my-example.png           ← One file per example (any format)
│
└── .github/
    ├── ISSUE_TEMPLATE/
    │   └── example-submission.yml   ← Structured submission form
    ├── workflows/
    │   └── process-submission.yml   ← GitHub Action (auto-PR on approval)
    └── scripts/
        └── parse_submission.py      ← Parses issue form → YAML entry
```

---

## First-Time Setup

### Step 1 — Update the submit URL

In `dashboard.qmd`, find this line in the R code chunk and replace
`YOUR-ORG/YOUR-REPO` with your actual GitHub organisation and repository name:

```r
submit_url <- paste0(
  "https://github.com/YOUR-ORG/YOUR-REPO",
  "/issues/new?template=example-submission.yml"
)
```

### Step 2 — Install the required R package

The dashboard uses the `yaml` package to read `examples.yaml`. Install it once:

```r
install.packages("yaml")
```

Your `renv.lock` (if you use `renv`) should capture this automatically when you
next run `renv::snapshot()`.

### Step 3 — Create a placeholder image

The placeholder image is shown on cards where no image has been provided.
A minimal SVG is included (`content/card-images/placeholder.svg`).
You can replace it with your own branded placeholder — any common image format
works (SVG, PNG, JPG).

### Step 4 — Configure `_quarto.yml`

A minimal Quarto website configuration:

```yaml
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs

website:
  title: "Your Project Name"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: dashboard.qmd
        text: Examples
      - href: instructions.qmd
        text: Guide

format:
  html:
    theme: default
```

### Step 5 — Configure GitHub Pages

In your repository: **Settings → Pages → Source → Deploy from branch → `main` → `/docs`.**

After this, every push to `main` that includes a re-rendered `docs/` folder will
update the live site.

---

## Managing the Examples Data

### The YAML file

All examples live in **`content/examples.yaml`**. Each entry is one card on
the dashboard. The file begins with a detailed comment header — read it before
making edits.

#### Field reference

| Field | Required | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `id` | ✅ | integer | Unique. Always increment from the last entry. |
| `title` | ✅ | string | Short title for the card (~60 chars). |
| `description` | ✅ | string | 1–3 sentences. Use the `>` block scalar for long text. |
| `url` | ✅ | string | Full public URL. |
| `tools` | ✅ | list | One or more tool categories (see below). |
| `keywords` | ✅ | list | One or more keywords (see below). |
| `image` | — | string | Filename only (e.g. `my-example.png`). File must be in `content/card-images/`. Leave blank `""` for placeholder. |
| `author` | — | string | Person or organisation. |
| `date_added` | — | string | ISO date `YYYY-MM-DD`. For internal tracking. |

#### Example entry

```yaml
- id: 7
  title: "My Open Science Workflow"
  description: >
    A reproducible end-to-end workflow for open science data analysis,
    from raw data ingestion through to publication-ready figures.
  url: "https://github.com/example/open-workflow"
  tools:
    - Reproducible Reports
    - Data Management
  keywords:
    - r
    - GitHub
    - template
  image: "open-workflow.png"
  author: "Jane Smith"
  date_added: "2025-03-01"
```

#### The YAML list syntax means no quoting nightmares

Unlike CSV, multi-value fields are just YAML lists — no need to wrap
comma-separated values in quotes. Each tool or keyword is its own indented
`- item` line.

---

### Adding an example manually

1. Open `content/examples.yaml` in RStudio or any text editor.
2. Copy an existing entry block and paste it at the bottom of the `examples:` list.
3. Increment `id` by 1 from the previous last entry.
4. Fill in the fields.
5. If you have an image, save it in `content/card-images/`.
6. Render (`quarto render`) and verify the card appears correctly.
7. Commit and push.

---

### Adding or modifying Tools and Keywords

Tools and keywords **do not need to be registered anywhere** — the dashboard
derives its dropdown options automatically from all values present in
`examples.yaml` at render time.

To **add** a new tool or keyword: use it in a new (or existing) entry.
It will appear in the dropdown on the next render.

To **remove** a tool or keyword from the dropdowns: remove it from every entry
that uses it.

To **rename** a tool or keyword: do a find-and-replace across `examples.yaml`.

The current default values are listed in the GitHub issue form template
(`example-submission.yml`) — update that file too when you add new official
tools or keywords so submitters see the full list.

---

## The Submission Workflow

### End-to-end flow

```
1. Community member opens the GitHub issue using the "Submit a New
   Dashboard Example" template and fills in the structured form.

2. The issue is auto-labelled "example-submission" and "needs-review".

3. Admin reviews the issue content.

4. If approved, admin adds the "approved" label.
       ↓
   GitHub Action fires automatically:
     a. Parses the issue form body
     b. Appends a new entry to content/examples.yaml
     c. Opens a Pull Request on a branch named example/issue-N

5. Admin reviews the PR:
     a. Checks the new YAML entry looks correct
     b. Downloads any submitted image → saves to content/card-images/
     c. Verifies any new tool/keyword values are intentional
     d. Runs quarto preview locally (optional but recommended)

6. Admin merges the PR.

7. Admin re-renders and pushes docs/ (or CI does it automatically).

8. New card appears live on the dashboard. ✅
```

---

### Setting up the GitHub Action (one-time)

The workflow file (`.github/workflows/process-submission.yml`) is already
in place. It needs two things before it will work:

#### Create the required issue labels

Go to your repo → **Issues → Labels → New label** and create:

| Label | Colour | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `example-submission` | any | Auto-applied by the issue template. Identifies submission issues. |
| `needs-review` | yellow | Auto-applied. Signals it's awaiting admin review. |
| `approved` | green | Applied **manually by admins** to trigger the Action. |
| `auto-generated` | grey | Applied to PRs created by the Action. |

#### Set workflow permissions

Go to: **Settings → Actions → General → Workflow permissions**
Select: **Read and write permissions** → Save.

This allows the Action to commit to the repository and open PRs using
`GITHUB_TOKEN` (no additional secrets needed).

---

### Reviewing and approving a submission (admin steps)

1. Open the new issue (labelled `example-submission` + `needs-review`).
2. Review: is the URL valid and publicly accessible? Is the description accurate?
   Are the tool/keyword selections appropriate?
3. If you want to reject it, close the issue with a comment explaining why.
4. If you approve it, add the **`approved`** label. The Action will run within ~1 minute.
5. Check the **Actions** tab to confirm the workflow completed successfully.
6. Find the new PR (it's linked in a comment automatically posted to the issue).
7. In the PR, review `content/examples.yaml` in the diff.
8. If the submitter provided an image URL:
   - Download the image.
   - Rename it to match the `image` field in the new YAML entry.
   - Add it to `content/card-images/` and commit it to the PR branch.
9. Merge the PR.
10. Re-render and push (`quarto render` → `git add docs/` → `git push`).

---

### Manual fallback (no GitHub Action)

If you prefer not to use the Action, or if something fails:

1. Read the issue carefully and extract the submitted values.
2. Open `content/examples.yaml` and manually append a new entry.
3. If an image was provided, download it to `content/card-images/`.
4. Commit, push, re-render.

---

## Rendering and Deployment

### Local render and preview

```bash
# In your project directory (or use RStudio's Render button):
quarto render

# Live preview in browser with hot reload:
quarto preview
```

### Deploying to GitHub Pages

The standard workflow after any content update:

```bash
quarto render          # rebuilds docs/
git add docs/
git commit -m "render: update dashboard"
git push
```

GitHub Pages will serve the updated `docs/` automatically within ~30 seconds.

### Automating renders with a GitHub Action (optional)

Add a second workflow to render the site automatically on every push to `main`:

```yaml
# .github/workflows/render-site.yml
name: Render and Deploy Site
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: r-lib/actions/setup-r@v2
        with:
          use-public-rspm: true

      - uses: r-lib/actions/setup-r-dependencies@v2
        with:
          packages: |
            any::tidyverse
            any::here
            any::yaml
            any::jsonlite

      - uses: quarto-dev/quarto-actions/setup@v2

      - name: Render site
        run: quarto render

      - uses: JamesIves/github-pages-deploy-action@v4
        with:
          folder: docs
```

---

## Customising the Dashboard

### Theme colours and fonts

All visual tokens are CSS custom properties at the top of `styles/dashboard.css`.
Change these to retheme the entire dashboard:

```css
:root {
  --ink:        #1a1410;   /* primary text */
  --ivory:      #f7f3ec;   /* page background */
  --terra:      #c4583a;   /* tool tag accent */
  --teal:       #2a6b6e;   /* keyword tag accent */
  --serif:      "Fraunces", Georgia, serif;
  --sans:       "DM Sans", system-ui, sans-serif;
  --mono:       "DM Mono", monospace;
}
```

To swap fonts: update the Google Fonts `<link>` tag in the `include-in-header`
section of `dashboard.qmd`, and update the `--serif`/`--sans`/`--mono` variables.

### Changing filter logic (OR → AND)

By default, selecting multiple tools uses **OR logic**: a card appears if it
matches *any* selected tool. To require *all* selected tools to match (AND logic),
edit the `filtering` OJS cell in `dashboard.qmd`:

```javascript
// Change .some() to .every() for AND logic:
const toolMatch =
  selectedTools.length === 0 ||
  selectedTools.every(t => exTools.includes(t))   // ← every, not some

const kwMatch =
  selectedKeywords.length === 0 ||
  selectedKeywords.every(k => exKws.includes(k))  // ← every, not some
```

### Adding a new field to cards

1. Add the new field to entries in `examples.yaml`.
2. In the R chunk in `dashboard.qmd`, add the field to the `map_dfr()` tibble.
3. In the OJS `cards` cell, reference `ex.your_field` inside `renderCard()`.

### Updating the issue form when tools/keywords change

When you add new official tools or keywords to `examples.yaml`, also add them
to the checkboxes in `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/example-submission.yml` so that
submitters can select them. The file is self-documented with comments.

---

## Troubleshooting

**Dashboard shows no examples / blank page**
: Confirm `content/examples.yaml` exists and is valid YAML (no tab characters —
use spaces). Run `yaml::read_yaml("content/examples.yaml")` in R to check.
Verify the `here()` path resolves correctly from your project root.

**Images not appearing on cards**
: The filename in the `image` field must exactly match the file in
`content/card-images/` (case-sensitive on Linux/GitHub). The card silently
falls back to the placeholder on load failure — open your browser's DevTools
console to see the actual 404 URL.

**New tools/keywords not appearing in dropdowns**
: The dropdowns are built from the data at render time. Re-render after editing
the YAML.

**GitHub Action not firing on "approved" label**
: Check the label name is spelled exactly `approved` (case-sensitive).
Check the Actions tab for errors. Confirm workflow permissions are set to
"Read and write" in repository Settings.

**`parse_submission.py` producing empty or wrong field values**
: GitHub renders issue form fields as `### Field Label\n\nvalue`. If GitHub
changes this format, the regex in the script may need updating. Print
`ISSUE_BODY` in the Action's logs to inspect the raw text, then adjust
the `extract_field()` patterns accordingly.

**`renv` doesn't include the `yaml` package**
: Run `install.packages("yaml")` then `renv::snapshot()` to capture it.

---

## Contributing to This Template

If you improve this dashboard template and want to share changes upstream,
please open an issue or PR in the original template repository.
Suggestions for new default tools/keywords, design improvements,
workflow enhancements, and accessibility fixes are all welcome.
